content: Taste is not an innate gift but a learnable discipline with three distinct components: Inputs (the quality and diversity of what you deliberately seek out), Filters (what you consciously block to protect your unique perspective from homogenization), and Discernment (the choices you make when translating absorbed influences into output). Most people focus only on the input side — consuming more — without developing the filter or discernment muscles that actually convert consumption into a distinctive point of view. In a world where AI can generate infinite content, the person with cultivated taste becomes the scarcest and most valuable node in any creative system.
implication: Builders and creators should treat taste development as a deliberate practice, not a passive byproduct of experience. Audit your inputs, build active filters against homogenizing forces, and practice high-stakes discernment decisions to sharpen the judgment that no tool can replicate.
source_ref: Where Scott Belsky Got His Good Taste — interview/video

content: The filter component of taste is arguably the hardest to develop because it runs counter to the internet-era instinct to consume everything. Belsky argues that preserving a unique lens requires actively blocking certain influences — not out of ignorance, but out of intentional self-protection. If you absorb too much of what already exists, your output becomes a remix of the consensus rather than a genuine original contribution. The creative with the most original perspective is often the one who has been most selective about what they allow in.
implication: High-output creative professionals should regularly audit what they consume and deliberately restrict exposure to dominant aesthetic trends in their own field, seeking inputs from adjacent or entirely unrelated domains instead.
source_ref: Where Scott Belsky Got His Good Taste — interview/video

content: Belsky frames the development of taste as analogous to maintaining a nutritious diet for the mind. Just as physical health requires not only eating well but also avoiding junk food, creative health requires both seeking high-quality diverse inputs and blocking the equivalent of creative junk food — the templated, algorithmic, trend-chasing content that floods every feed. A steady diet of the median produces median sensibility, and median sensibility produces forgettable work.
implication: Product designers, founders, and creative leaders should be as intentional about what they don't consume as what they do — treating low-quality, trend-driven content as a genuine threat to their creative immune system.
source_ref: Adobe's Scott Belsky: Art & design in the age of AI | Rapid Response

content: In a world saturated with AI-generated content, originality and craft become the ultimate competitive differentiators — not despite abundance, but because of it. Belsky draws a direct parallel to how fast food didn't eliminate fine dining; if anything, the ubiquity of industrialized food elevated the cultural and economic value of craft cuisine. AI-generated slop will follow the same pattern: the more it floods the zone, the more the market will re-price genuine originality upward.
implication: Creators and product teams should resist the temptation to compete on volume or speed with AI tools. The winning strategy is to double down on the things that are genuinely hard to replicate — idiosyncratic perspective, earned sensibility, and deliberate craft.
source_ref: Adobe's Scott Belsky: Art & design in the age of AI | Rapid Response

content: Belsky argues that deliberate imperfections and signs of human hand will become premium signals in an AI-saturated world. When perfection is algorithmically cheap, the handmade and the idiosyncratic become scarce — and scarcity is what markets price. The unpolished, the personal, the slightly irregular: these are not flaws to be optimized away but proof-of-humanity markers that communicate authenticity in a way a generative model structurally cannot.
implication: Creators should resist the instinct to use AI tools to polish their work to a frictionless sheen. Leaving traces of human judgment, imperfect choices, and personal idiosyncrasy is increasingly a strategic asset, not a liability.
source_ref: 2026 Predictions with Scott Belsky — video

content: The proliferation of templates — in design tools, in business decks, in marketing copy — homogenizes output across the entire market. When everyone uses the same starting point, the outputs converge toward a shared aesthetic median that belongs to no one. Belsky sees template dependency as one of the primary threats to originality in the current era, because it allows speed and competence without cultivating the underlying taste that would make work genuinely distinctive.
implication: Teams and creators should treat templates as scaffolding to be discarded, not finished floors to live on. The template gets you to competent; the taste gets you to memorable. Invest in developing the discernment to know when to deviate.
source_ref: Building Adobe & the Future of Design with Scott Belsky — video

content: Belsky has consistently argued that creativity is the new productivity — not in a metaphorical sense but as a literal reallocation of human energy. As AI absorbs more of the execution layer, the comparative advantage of human workers shifts entirely toward the creative and curatorial: deciding what to make, why it matters, and what it should feel like. The worker who optimizes for output quantity is competing with machines; the worker who optimizes for creative judgment is complementing them.
implication: Professionals across every function — not just designers and writers — should be actively investing in their creative sensibility. In the AI era, creative judgment is not a soft skill for artists; it is the core professional skill for everyone.
source_ref: Interview with Scott Belsky: GenAI - Creativity as the Now Productivity

content: Taste in product design manifests most clearly in the choices that never get articulated in a spec or a roadmap — the micro-decisions about spacing, sequencing, tone, and restraint that collectively determine whether something feels considered or accidental. Belsky emphasizes that product craft is not decoration applied after the functional work is done; it is woven into every decision at every layer. Teams that treat craft as a final polish phase will always produce work that feels like an afterthought.
implication: Product leaders should embed craft decisions into the earliest stages of product definition, not the final stages of production. What the product omits, how it responds, and what it refuses to do are as much expressions of taste as any visual choice.
source_ref: The Messy Middle — book

content: Belsky's position is that you should never MVP the thing that defines you. Cutting corners on your core differentiator teaches the market nothing about your actual conviction and trains early users to expect mediocrity at precisely the moment their impressions are being formed. The MVP philosophy is sound for peripheral features and unproven hypotheses — but applying it to the thing that makes you different is a form of strategic self-sabotage masquerading as pragmatism.
implication: Founders must draw a hard line between the features that can be iterated cheaply and the core craft proposition that defines why the company exists. Ship fast on everything else; be uncompromising on the thing that represents your genuine creative bet.
source_ref: Lessons on product sense, AI, the first mile experience, and the messy middle | Scott Belsky (Adobe)

content: Belsky traces his own taste development to a period of deliberately cross-disciplinary exposure — studying organizational theory at Cornell, then working in finance at Goldman Sachs, before founding Behance in the creative world. Each domain contributed a different lens: systems thinking, financial rigor, and aesthetic sensibility. His argument is that taste is rarely forged within a single domain — it emerges from the productive collision of multiple frameworks applied to a new problem.
implication: Aspiring creative leaders should seek out experiences that are deliberately out-of-field, not just laterally adjacent. The strangest combinations of background — engineering and poetry, finance and visual art — tend to produce the most original sensibilities.
source_ref: Where Scott Belsky Got His Good Taste — interview/video

content: Belsky argues that the scarcest resource in the coming decade is not intelligence, compute, or even capital — it is knowing what to make. AI can generate infinite variations on existing things; it cannot originate genuine need, identify genuine beauty, or feel genuine disgust at what is wrong with the world. The human who can do those three things — and act on them — is not just surviving the AI transition; they are the reason the transition produces anything worth having.
implication: Teams and investors should be evaluating creative vision and taste as primary leadership criteria, not secondary cultural ones. The ability to know what to make — and what not to make — is now a core business capability, not an artistic luxury.
source_ref: Adobe's Scott Belsky: Art & design in the age of AI | Rapid Response

content: Belsky has noted that the prompt era of AI creative tools is a transitional interface, not the final form. The next evolution gives creators direct, tactile manipulation controls that feel like natural extensions of their existing craft practice — not a blank box demanding the right incantation. This shift matters enormously for taste and craft because prompt-based interfaces reward those who can describe what they want, while control-based interfaces reward those who can feel and sculpt their way to what they want.
implication: Creative professionals should not optimize for prompt engineering as a long-term skill. The competitive advantage will belong to those who develop deep aesthetic intuition that can be expressed through direct manipulation — the same intuition that defined mastery in pre-AI craft domains.
source_ref: Building Adobe & the Future of Design with Scott Belsky — video

content: One of Belsky's most consistent positions is that originality requires a willingness to be misunderstood early. The work that looks strange or premature to contemporaries is often the work that ages best, because it wasn't calibrated to match existing taste but to push past it. He connects this directly to his observation that the talent most likely to define a company's next chapter is often the talent that the current chapter doesn't know what to do with — the same dynamic applies to ideas.
implication: Creative leaders should build explicit tolerance for work that generates internal discomfort or skepticism before external validation. If everything your team produces receives immediate consensus approval, you are probably producing consensus-level work.
source_ref: The Messy Middle — book

content: Belsky draws a sharp distinction between being inspired by the work of others and being captured by it. The former is healthy — a nutritious input that expands your range of possibility. The latter is dangerous — a gravitational pull that slowly colonizes your aesthetic until your output is a sophisticated derivative of someone else's vision. Developing genuine taste requires recognizing the moment inspiration tips into imitation and having the discipline to pull back toward your own instincts.
implication: Creatives and product leaders should periodically ask whether their aesthetic choices are expressions of their own evolving sensibility or echoes of whoever they most recently admired. The answer reveals whether taste is being built or borrowed.
source_ref: Where Scott Belsky Got His Good Taste — interview/video

content: Belsky has argued that craft is most clearly expressed not in what a product includes but in what it refuses to include. The discipline of omission — removing features, simplifying flows, stripping unnecessary explanations — is harder than the discipline of addition and far more revealing of genuine taste. Most teams add because addition feels like progress; the teams with the strongest aesthetic conviction subtract because they understand that restraint is the signature of mastery.
implication: Product reviews and design critiques should spend as much time evaluating what was intentionally excluded as what was included. The quality of the 'no' decisions is one of the most reliable indicators of taste at the team level.
source_ref: The Messy Middle — book

content: Belsky has positioned Behance's founding mission — creating a meritocracy based on quality of work rather than credentials or connections — as itself a statement about taste and craft. The insight was that the creative world had a systemic dysfunction: the best work wasn't winning because it wasn't being seen. Building the infrastructure for creative visibility was, in his framing, an act of taste — a bet that quality, made legible and accessible, would displace gatekeeping as the organizing principle of creative careers.
implication: Platforms and organizations that create genuine meritocracies of craft — where quality of work, not pedigree or proximity, determines visibility — are making a structural bet on taste. That bet compounds when AI raises the floor of competent execution and makes the ceiling of genuine craft even more valuable.
source_ref: TEDxGotham 2011 - Scott Belsky - Making a Creative Meritocracy

content: Belsky frames the development of creative discernment as inseparable from the accumulation of failures in judgment. You cannot develop taste without making bad choices, feeling the wrongness of them, and recalibrating. The shortcut that most people take — following existing rules, copying successful precedents, deferring to consensus — produces technical competence but not genuine discernment, because discernment is built through the lived experience of being wrong about what was good.
implication: Creative education and mentorship should create safe environments for poor taste to be expressed and examined, not environments where it is corrected before it is even articulated. You learn discernment by making calls, not by watching someone else make them.
source_ref: Scott Belsky: Lessons from the Messy Middle of a Startup's Journey

content: Belsky sees the convergence of art, design, and technology not as a dilution of craft but as its expansion. At Adobe, his position has been that the tools enabling more people to express their creative vision do not threaten professional craft — they raise the ambient standard and make genuinely extraordinary craft more visible by contrast. The rising floor of competent production creates a sharper distinction at the top, not a flatter one.
implication: Professional creators should welcome, not fear, the democratization of creative tools. The response to a raised floor is not to lobby for the floor to stay low — it is to raise your own ceiling, which requires investing in the taste and discernment that tools cannot supply.
source_ref: Adobe Blog: Enriching the Creative Experience — essay

content: Belsky argues that the most dangerous moment for a creator's originality is early success — because the market rewards the thing you already did, creating enormous pressure to reproduce it. The creator who has developed strong taste filters can recognize this gravitational pull and resist it; the creator who hasn't will spend years producing diminishing variations of their own breakthrough. Originality requires actively choosing discomfort over the comfort of doing again what already worked.
implication: Successful creators and product teams should build explicit review processes that ask whether new work is advancing their vision or replicating their last success. Repeating yourself is not craft — it is brand management masquerading as creativity.
source_ref: The Messy Middle — book

content: In Belsky's framework, creative constraints are not enemies of craft but generators of it. Behance itself was built under extreme resource constraints, and he has consistently argued that the discipline forced by scarcity produces more original solutions than abundance ever does, because it prevents the lazy option. A team with unlimited budget and time will converge on the obvious answer; a team with neither must find a genuinely novel path.
implication: Leaders who want more original creative output from their teams should experiment with artificial constraints — tighter timelines, narrower scope, reduced toolsets — as a way of forcing the creative problem-solving that abundance tends to suppress.
source_ref: #11: Behance Founder, Scott Belsky, on maximizing creative output and the role constraints play

content: Belsky has observed that the creative professionals who build the most distinctive bodies of work are those who treat their personal obsessions as primary research, not as distractions to be managed. The frustration that led to Behance's founding — a felt problem in the world that no existing solution adequately addressed — is the same signal he advises creators to follow. The work that matters almost always begins with something the creator personally couldn't stop caring about.
implication: Creators should mine their own dissatisfactions and obsessions as the most reliable source of original work. The project born from genuine personal frustration has an authentic energy that research-derived insights rarely produce — and an audience built from the implicit promise of solving a real felt problem.
source_ref: TEDxPugetSound - Scott Belsky - Making Ideas Happen

content: Belsky's thesis on craft in the AI era is ultimately about reclaiming the things that have always mattered but were temporarily obscured by the efficiency obsession of the digital era. Speed, scale, and optimization are table stakes — AI makes them cheap. The differentiators that remain are the same ones that defined great craft before industrialization: care, intention, selectivity, and the willingness to spend more time on something than the market strictly requires.
implication: Teams should recalibrate their definition of productivity in the AI era. The relevant question is no longer how quickly you can produce something acceptable, but how deeply you can develop the taste and craft to produce something genuinely worth having.
source_ref: Interview with Scott Belsky: GenAI - Creativity as the Now Productivity

content: Belsky notes that taste is ultimately expressed through a long sequence of micro-decisions, each of which appears inconsequential in isolation — a word choice, a color, a pause, a line break — but which accumulate into a distinctive sensibility that is recognizable and hard to reverse-engineer. This is precisely why taste is a durable moat: it cannot be copied wholesale because it is not a set of rules but a lived practice of judgment. You can imitate the outputs of someone's taste; you cannot replicate the internalized process that produced them.
implication: Organizations that want to build taste as an institutional capability must invest in the long, slow accumulation of micro-decision practice — not taste audits or aesthetic guidelines, but the daily discipline of making and evaluating thousands of small choices with intentionality.
source_ref: Lessons on product sense, AI, the first mile experience, and the messy middle | Scott Belsky (Adobe)
